title: Europe’s missile defence gap: is South Korea part of the answer?
gist: Europe's air- and missile-defence demand has surged while a strained, Indo-Pacific-focused US becomes a less reliable supplier; South Korea, already the second-largest arms supplier to European NATO members, is well placed to fill part of the gap, though EU localisation rules limit its access.

insights.claim: European air and missile defence has returned to the top of the security agenda since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, with stockpiles depleted as Germany, the Netherlands, Romania and Spain transferred Raytheon Patriot systems to Ukraine.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Depleted European Patriot stocks create durable replacement demand; structurally bullish for air-defence prime contractors.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'Stockpiles have been markedly reduced'

insights.claim: European governments have spent roughly US$50 billion since 2022 on air and missile defence, primarily on US systems, after the 2025 Readiness 2030 White Paper named it one of seven critical capability gaps.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A $50bn+ spend pool is mostly flowing to US vendors today, defining the contestable market new entrants are targeting.
insights.quote: European governments have spent heavily – reportedly around US$50 billion since 2022 – primarily on US systems.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'To meet urgent needs'

insights.claim: Europe's shortfall is most acute in medium- and long-range ballistic-missile defence, since NASAMS and IRIS-T SLM counter cruise missiles and drones but not ballistic threats, and SAMP/T has limited production capacity.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The specific gap is high-tier ballistic-missile defence, not air defence broadly; that is where supply scarcity and pricing power concentrate.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'The resulting capability shortfall'

insights.claim: Washington's strategic focus on the Indo-Pacific and absorption in the Middle East has reduced Europe to at best a tertiary security interest, undercutting reliance on US missile-defence supply.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: US prioritisation makes Europe a residual customer; a structural opening for non-US suppliers to win European defence share.
insights.quote: Europe has become, at best, a tertiary security interest.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'With Washington’s long-term strategic focus'

insights.claim: Trump's NATO scepticism, tensions over Europe's response to US strikes on Iran, and reported plans to withdraw ~5,000 US troops from Germany are seeding doubt about Washington as a security guarantor.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Political risk on US commitment accelerates European supplier diversification; a tailwind for European and allied defence industries.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'President Donald Trump’s long-standing scepticism'

insights.claim: US interceptor inventories are depleted: up to 20% of SM-3 and 20-50% of Lockheed Martin THAAD missiles were expended in the June 2025 Iran-Israel conflict, with Operation Epic Fury further drawing down stocks in February 2026.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Hard depletion data means the US will replenish itself first; supply scarcity is quantified, not speculative.
insights.quote: Up to 20% of Washington’s SM-3 interceptors and 20–50% of Lockheed Martin Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) missiles were expended by the US in the June 2025 conflict
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'Even without political friction'

insights.claim: Gulf countries expended approximately 800 Patriot interceptors while Lockheed Martin produced only 620 in 2025, illustrating that demand is outrunning production and that Gulf partners may take export priority over Europe.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Annual Patriot output is below single-conflict consumption; the production-demand mismatch is the core scarcity thesis and Europe is at the back of the queue.
insights.quote: Lockheed Martin produced 620 Patriot missile interceptors in 2025.
insights.locator: Europe's missile-defence gap section, 'Gulf countries expended approximately 800 Patriot'

insights.claim: South Korea was the second-largest supplier of major arms to European NATO members in 2021-25 behind only the US, having delivered tanks, howitzers and aircraft to Poland and signed K9/K10 and Chunmoo contracts with Romania, Norway and Estonia.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Korea already has a proven European track record and delivery speed; a credible alternative supplier as US reliability erodes.
insights.quote: South Korea was the second-largest supplier of major arms to European NATO members during the 2021–25 period, behind only the US.
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'According to a 2026 report'

insights.claim: South Korea's Cheongung-II surface-to-air system, made by LIG Nex1, Hanwha Systems and Hanwha Aerospace, reportedly achieved a 96%+ interception rate after UAE deployment in early 2026, with pending orders from Saudi Arabia and Iraq.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Combat-proven performance plus Gulf demand strengthens Korea's export case; watch Hanwha and LIG Nex1 as missile-defence beneficiaries.
insights.quote: reportedly achieved an interception success rate exceeding 96%
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'Two systems were deployed in early 2026'

insights.claim: South Korean systems are roughly 95% domestically produced, reducing supply-chain and export-restriction vulnerability while keeping production costs low.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: High domestic content insulates Korea from the supply-chain chokes plaguing US output; a structural competitive edge for buyers seeking resilience.
insights.quote: their high degree of domestic production – estimated at around 95% – which reduces vulnerability to supply-chain disruptions
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'One advantage of South Korea’s systems'

insights.claim: South Korea's L-SAM, described as a Korean THAAD equivalent, is expected ready for deployment by 2028, potentially extending Korea into higher-tier missile defence.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: By 2028 Korea could compete in the high-tier ballistic-defence segment where Europe's gap is most acute; a forward catalyst to track.
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'South Korea’s L-SAM system'

insights.claim: Hanwha is expanding production in Poland and Romania, and although these plants are not currently for interceptors, they could be adapted to shorten delivery timelines for European customers.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Local European production could neutralise the localisation barrier and speed delivery; a strategic move to watch in Korea's EU push.
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'Hanwha is expanding production in Europe'

insights.claim: The EU's SAFE initiative, adopted May 2025, allocates €150bn but requires at least 65% of a product to be EU-produced to qualify for loans, effectively excluding South Korean systems from SAFE-funded procurement.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The 65% EU-content rule is the key gatekeeper limiting Korean access; local production or partnership is the workaround to monitor.
insights.quote: projects must meet a requirement that at least 65% of the product is produced within the EU
insights.locator: Can South Korea help section, 'The European Union’s Security Action for Europe'

insights.claim: Europe faces a dilemma between rebuilding its own defence-industrial base for autonomy and filling immediate missile-defence gaps, with external partners like South Korea best placed to help in the near term.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Near-term gap-filling and long-term autonomy pull in opposite directions; expect pragmatic Korean buys alongside protectionist EU funding rules.
insights.quote: In the near term, external partners such as South Korea would be well placed to help Europe enhance readiness and reduce reliance on the US.
insights.locator: Striking a balance section, 'Europe faces a dilemma'
